title: Interfaces should not be empty
description: Interfaces should not be emptyAnalyze your codeconsistency-conventionalmaintainabilityCode SmellEmpty interfaces should be avoided as they do not provide any functional requirements for implementing classes.Why is this an issue?How can I fix it?More InfoEmpty interfaces are either useless or used as amarker.Custom attributesare a better alternative to marker interfaces. See theHow to fix itsection for more information.ExceptionsThis rule doesn’t raise in any of the following cases:Aggregation of multiple interfacespublic interface IAggregate: IComparable, IFormattable { } // Compliant: Aggregates two interfacesGeneric specializationAn empty interface with a single base interface is compliant as long as the resulting interface binds a generic parameter or constrains it.// Compliant: Bound t
recommendation: Refactor to eliminate the flagged pattern.

compliant:
// Compliant: marker interface that specializes generic types
public interface IAggregate<TId> : IAggregate { }

// Compliant: marker interface for framework requirements
public interface IEntity : IComparable, IFormattable { }

logic.detection_conditions: Detects interfaces with no members that don't inherit from other interfaces or specialize generics
logic.triggering_conditions: (InterfaceDeclaration with empty body AND no base interfaces)
exclusion_conditions: Interfaces that aggregate multiple interfaces (inherit from 2+ interfaces)
Interfaces with at least one member (method, property, event)
never_trigger_on: Interfaces inheriting from multiple base interfaces
logic.reasoning: Empty interfaces provide no contract and are considered code smell. This rule detects InterfaceDeclaration nodes that have empty bodies (no methods, properties, or events) and don't serve as aggregators or generic specializers. Exceptions are made for interfaces that inherit from multiple bases (aggregation) or bind generic parameters (specialization), as these serve legitimate architectural purposes.
